one_line_takeaway: A longitudinal red-team study of 8 MLLM releases (4 vendors x 2 generations) using a fixed 726-prompt, 82,256-annotation benchmark finds that MLLM safety does not improve monotonically across model updates -- GPT and Claude both got measurably more jailbreakable (+8%/+10% ASR) even as Pixtral and Qwen improved (-7%/-5% ASR), and Claude's apparent safety lead is substantially a refusal-rate artifact rather than more robust generation.
key_finding: MLLM safety is not monotonically improving across model generations and is both family-specific and modality-specific. Cross-generation ASR deltas (Section 4.3): GPT models +8% overall ASR (driven by +18% multimodal ASR increase, partly offset by a -4% text-only decrease); Claude models +10% overall ASR despite remaining the family with the lowest expected-harmfulness scores; Pixtral models -7% overall ASR while still the highest-risk family in both phases; Qwen models -5% overall ASR. In Phase 1 every model was more vulnerable to text-only than multimodal prompts (overall ASR 0.35 text vs 0.31 multimodal), but in Phase 2 this pattern became model-dependent: GPT-5 (ASR 0.274 multimodal vs 0.262 text-only) and Claude Sonnet 4.5 (~0.19 vs ~0.20) showed near-parity across modalities, Qwen Omni was similarly balanced (0.354 vs 0.335), while Pixtral Large retained a strong text-only elevation (0.60 text vs 0.50 multimodal). The regression's fixed effects (Table D2) show Pixtral 12B (beta=3.81, SE=0.06) and Qwen VL Plus (beta=2.96, SE=0.06) with much higher harm-rating log-odds than the GPT-4o reference level, Claude Sonnet 3.5 lower (beta=-0.37, SE=0.07), and text-only modality strongly increasing harm ratings overall (beta=2.10, SE=0.06), all p<0.001, with significant model x modality interactions confirming that modality sensitivity does not generalize across model generations. A second key finding is methodological: Claude models had the highest default-refusal rates in both phases, which the authors argue drives their low harmfulness scores rather than reflecting inherently safer generative behavior -- newer GPT and Claude models reduced refusal rates relative to predecessors, Pixtral showed only a slight refusal reduction, and Qwen Omni actually showed a higher refusal rate than Qwen VL Plus, so raw harmfulness comparisons across models conflate abstention strategy with generative safety.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is a two-phase, longitudinal red-teaming evaluation of eight commercially deployed multimodal LLM releases (four model families, one earlier and one later generation each) against a single fixed adversarial benchmark, designed to measure whether MLLM safety/harmlessness drifts across model updates rather than only at a single point in time. Phase 1 (an earlier, cited-as-'Anonymous 2025' prior study by the same authors) evaluated GPT-4o, Claude Sonnet 3.5, Pixtral 12B, and Qwen VL Plus; Phase 2, the new work reported here, re-ran the identical 726-prompt benchmark on the successor models GPT-5, Claude Sonnet 4.5, Pixtral Large, and Qwen Omni. Across both phases the authors collected 82,256 human harmfulness annotations (47,408 in Phase 1, 34,848 in Phase 2) over 5,808 model responses (2,904 per phase) and analyzed them with a mixed-effects regression model plus attack success rate (ASR).
threat_model: Adversarial prompting / red-teaming against the harmlessness alignment of multimodal LLMs accessed via official vendor APIs in a single-turn setting. The attack surface spans text-only and multimodal (image+text) inputs. 26 professional red teamers authored 726 prompts (363 text-only, 363 multimodal pairs, each multimodal prompt paired with an equivalent text-only variant to isolate modality effects) spanning three harm categories: illegal activity (n=334), disinformation (n=136), and unethical behaviour (n=256). Multimodal attacks used three execution strategies: harmful text embedded in images, benign images paired with harmful text, and harmful images paired with neutral/ambiguous captions. Named attack tactics (Appendix C) include role-play/hypothetical persona, response priming, strategic framing/persuasion, 'ignore instructions,' refusal suppression, and noise/distraction. This is a black-box, API-level evaluation threat model -- no white-box access to weights, training data, or moderation-layer internals.
limitations: Self-reported by the authors (Section 7): (1) API-only access provides no visibility into training data, alignment procedures, or moderation layers, so behavioral differences cannot be attributed to specific architectural or tuning mechanisms; (2) the adversarial dataset is fixed at 726 prompts across only three harm categories and may not reflect the full range of real-world jailbreak strategies or multi-step adversarial interactions; (3) all prompts and annotations were in U.S. English by native English speakers, so results may not generalize to multilingual/cross-cultural settings; (4) evaluation is single-turn only, not multi-turn dialogue; (5) multimodal prompts were limited to static images built from three predefined strategies, excluding audio, video, OCR-based, or dynamic cross-modal attacks; (6) refusal was flagged but not modeled as an independent outcome variable, limiting the ability to fully separate abstention-driven safety from alignment-driven safety; (7) the regression model's convergence was not fully achieved (max gradient = 0.704), a caveat the authors attach directly to their own coefficient estimates. Reviewer-identified grounding issue (not authors' own limitation): the paper reports materially different ASR numbers for the same model/modality cells in the narrative Results text (Section 4.1/4.2) versus Appendix Table D1 -- e.g., body text gives Pixtral 12B ASR as 0.72 multimodal/0.63 text-only and Claude Sonnet 3.5 as 'below 0.03' in both modalities, while Table D1 lists Pixtral 12B at 0.61 multimodal/0.63 text-only and Claude Sonnet 3.5 at 0.05 multimodal/0.12 text-only; GPT-4o is given as 0.067 multimodal/0.043 text in the narrative vs. 0.09 multimodal/0.30 text-only in Table D1. This internal inconsistency was not flagged by the authors and should be treated as a caveat on any single ASR figure pulled from this paper.

datasets_used: Custom 726-prompt adversarial benchmark authored by 26 professional red teamers (363 text-only, 363 multimodal, paired 1:1 across modality)
Harm categories within the benchmark: illegal activities (n=334), disinformation (n=136), unethical activities (n=256)
82,256 total human harmfulness annotations across both phases (47,408 Phase 1 + 34,848 Phase 2) over 5,808 total model responses (2,904 per phase)
quantitative_results: Phase 1 narrative ASR: Pixtral 12B 0.72 multimodal / 0.63 text-only; Claude Sonnet 3.5 <0.03 both modalities; GPT-4o 0.067 multimodal / 0.043 text-only; Qwen VL Plus 0.36 multimodal / 0.32 text-only; overall Phase 1 ASR 0.35 text-only vs 0.31 multimodal

Phase 2 narrative ASR: Pixtral Large 0.60 text-only / 0.50 multimodal; Claude Sonnet 4.5 ~0.19-0.20 both modalities; GPT-5 0.274 multimodal / 0.262 text-only; Qwen Omni 0.354 multimodal / 0.335 text-only
Cross-generation ASR change: GPT models +8% overall (multimodal +18%, text-only -4%); Claude models +10% overall; Pixtral models -7% overall; Qwen models -5% overall

Appendix Table D1 ASR (differs from narrative text, see limitations): GPT-4o 0.19 individual / 0.09 multimodal / 0.30 text-only; GPT-5 0.27 / 0.27 / 0.26; Claude Sonnet 3.5 0.09 / 0.05 / 0.12; Claude Sonnet 4.5 0.19 / 0.19 / 0.20; Pixtral 12B 0.62 / 0.61 / 0.63; Pixtral Large 0.55 / 0.50 / 0.60; Qwen VL Plus 0.39 / 0.47 / 0.31; Qwen Omni 0.34 / 0.35 / 0.33; overall 0.33 / 0.31 / 0.35

Regression fixed effects vs. GPT-4o reference (Table D2): Pixtral 12B beta=3.81 (SE 0.06, p<0.001); Qwen VL Plus beta=2.96 (SE 0.06, p<0.001); Claude Sonnet 3.5 beta=-0.37 (SE 0.07, p<0.001); Claude Sonnet 4.5 beta=0.83 (SE 0.24, p=0.0004); GPT-5 beta=1.42 (SE 0.24, p<0.001); Pixtral Large beta=3.02 (SE 0.23, p<0.0001); Qwen Omni beta=2.27 (SE 0.24, p<0.0001); modality text-only beta=2.10 (SE 0.06, p<0.0001)

Model fit: N=82,256 observations, AIC=137,112.84, log-likelihood=-68,532.42, convergence not fully achieved (max gradient=0.704); random-effect variance: prompt=2.32 (SD 1.52), participant=0.36 (SD 0.60)
Annotator demographic covariates (gender, age group) were non-significant to marginal (all p>0.09), indicating model and modality, not annotator characteristics, drove harm ratings
baselines_compared: No external ML baseline/defense methods are compared; this is a comparative empirical study across 8 commercial MLLMs used as mutual reference points: GPT-4o vs GPT-5 (OpenAI), Claude Sonnet 3.5 vs Claude Sonnet 4.5 (Anthropic), Pixtral 12B vs Pixtral Large (Mistral), Qwen VL Plus vs Qwen Omni (Alibaba), with GPT-4o used as the reference level in the regression's fixed-effect coefficients
